narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel deeply disheartening amid the unique challenges you may have faced. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 5 and 4 respectively, upper-middle family income, and plan to attend full-time—against patterns from thousands of past students' bar exam outcomes. It predicted a 41.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for denying your application to prioritize applicants with lower predicted risks. While your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score were strengths—both above typical averages and the top factors pulling the prediction toward bar passage—your Year 3 school decile stood out as the biggest concern, significantly pushing the model toward expecting failure since it was below average. Your full-time enrollment plan helped a bit, aligning with the vast majority of successful students, but it wasn't enough to offset the Year 3 decile's strong negative pull. Lesser influences like your Year 1 decile and family income slightly nudged toward higher risk but paled compared to those main drivers. Overall, your profile showed solid academics in some areas that match or exceed what's typical, yet the dip in later-year performance raised red flags when stacked against students who passed the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a brilliant Black woman ready to advocate fiercely—models like this are just one data-driven tool schools use. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or strengthening recent grades through post-bac courses to boost your decile rankings, and target schools with holistic reviews that value your background. Reach out to mentors from similar walks who passed the bar; their stories could guide targeted improvements. You've got resilience—use this as fuel to reapply even stronger next cycle. We're rooting for you.
